draft.introduction: Matthew 13 gathers the parables of the kingdom: the sower, the wheat and tares, the mustard seed, the leaven. Each begins “The kingdom of heaven is like...”

translation.text: That same day Jesus went out of the house and sat beside the sea. And great crowds gathered about him. And he told them many things in parables, saying: “A sower went out to sow. And as he sowed, some seeds fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured them. Other seeds fell on rocky ground. Other seeds fell among thorns. Other seeds fell on good soil and produced grain, some a hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.”
translation.original_text: ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ἐξελθὼν ὁ Ἰησοῦς

translation.text: He put another parable before them: “The kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed that a man took and sowed in his field. It is the smallest of all seeds, but when it has grown it is larger than all the garden plants and becomes a tree.”

translator_notes.note: Jesus explains that parables simultaneously reveal and conceal: “To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom, but to them it has not been given.” This epistemological asymmetry troubled interpreters from Origen onward.
